title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about a chef who has dedicated his career to reviving forgotten regional food traditions. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Memory Kitchen
content.subtitle: A food magazine profile of chef Marco Ferrante and his quest to preserve culinary heritage.
content.text: Marco Ferrante's restaurant in a narrow street of Palermo's old quarter does not feature on the lists of fashionable Italian restaurants that food journalists compile each year. There is no tasting menu, no theatrical presentation, and no staff trained to recite the provenance of each ingredient. What there is, when you sit down, is a bowl of something that may well change your understanding of what Italian cooking can be. [31] 'I want people to eat something their great-grandmother might have made,' Ferrante told me, 'and to understand that they've been cheated by everything they thought was Italian food before.' [/31] || Ferrante spent fifteen years working in some of Europe's most celebrated fine dining kitchens before returning to Palermo in his mid-thirties with what his former colleagues considered an inexplicable ambition: to cook the food of Sicily's poor. [32] He was not interested in making poverty glamorous or quaint. He was interested in the ingredients and techniques that had been discarded when prosperity made it possible to eat differently — and in asking why those flavours had been abandoned so readily. [/32] || Much of his research was conducted not in libraries or food archives but in conversations with elderly women in rural towns across western Sicily. Some of them, he says, were initially suspicious. Chefs from cities, in their experience, came to take what was useful and package it as novelty. [33] Ferrante spent months simply visiting, eating what he was offered, and declining to take notes in their presence. Only when trust had been established did he begin to ask questions. [/33] || The result of these years of research is a menu that reads like a lost chapter of Mediterranean food history. Dishes built around ingredients — wild greens, preserved fish, dried legumes — that modern supermarkets barely stock, prepared with techniques, such as slow-cooking in terracotta vessels sealed with dough, that have no place in a professional kitchen equipped for speed. [34] Ferrante employs four cooks, all of them from Palermo, and refuses to take shortcuts, even when the restaurant is full and the kitchen is under pressure. [/34] 'If you make it fast, it becomes something else,' he says. 'The time is part of the recipe.' || The response from Palermo's food community was initially mixed. Some local critics praised his work as important cultural preservation. Others found his menu wilfully inaccessible — difficult to eat, difficult to understand without the kind of background that most diners simply do not bring to a meal. [35] Ferrante remains unbothered by this criticism. He keeps a copy of one particularly hostile review pinned to the kitchen wall, he tells me, not as a reminder of failure but as a standard of what he is not trying to be. [/35] || When I ask whether he worries that the traditions he is trying to preserve will disappear when the last generation of people who learned them passes on, he pauses. [36] 'That's why the restaurant exists,' he says quietly. 'Not to be a museum. To keep the thing alive, you have to keep cooking it.' [/36]

questions.question: Ferrante's comment that diners have 'been cheated' suggests that he believes
options: A) mainstream Italian restaurants charge too much for inferior food.
B) authentic regional cooking offers an experience that popular Italian food does not.
C) Italian cuisine has been deliberately misrepresented abroad for commercial reasons.
D) most people are unwilling to try genuinely traditional Italian dishes.
questions.explanation: Ferrante wants diners to experience something their great-grandmother might have made, implying that conventional 'Italian food' has not offered the genuine article — hence diners have been 'cheated' out of the real thing.

questions.question: What was Ferrante's actual motivation in choosing to cook the food of Sicily's poor?
options: A) To create a fashionable narrative around working-class culinary traditions.
B) To investigate what had been lost when prosperity changed people's eating habits.
C) To demonstrate that simple ingredients could rival those used in fine dining.
D) To challenge the dominance of northern Italian cuisine in the restaurant world.
questions.explanation: The writer explicitly states Ferrante was interested in 'ingredients and techniques that had been discarded when prosperity made it possible to eat differently' and in understanding why they were abandoned.

questions.question: Why were the elderly women initially suspicious of Ferrante?
options: A) They were afraid that their recipes might be published without permission.
B) They felt that chefs had previously exploited local food knowledge for profit.
C) They believed their traditional dishes were not good enough for a restaurant menu.
D) They had bad experiences with outsiders coming to their villages in the past.
questions.explanation: The text states that in the women's experience, 'chefs from cities came to take what was useful and package it as novelty' — showing previous exploitation of local knowledge.

questions.question: The description of Ferrante's kitchen techniques suggests that his main priority is
options: A) making the restaurant as financially successful as possible.
B) training young local cooks in endangered culinary traditions.
C) preserving the authenticity of the dishes regardless of operational efficiency.
D) creating dishes that can be enjoyed by diners with no prior knowledge of Sicilian food.
questions.explanation: Ferrante 'refuses to take shortcuts, even when the restaurant is full and the kitchen is under pressure', and says 'the time is part of the recipe' — prioritising authenticity over practicality.

questions.question: How does Ferrante respond to the hostile review pinned to his kitchen wall?
options: A) He uses it to motivate himself to improve his food.
B) He keeps it as a reminder that not everyone appreciates traditional cooking.
C) He treats it as a useful definition of what his restaurant is not trying to achieve.
D) He considers it evidence that critics are unsuited to judge his kind of work.
questions.explanation: Ferrante keeps the review 'not as a reminder of failure but as a standard of what he is not trying to be' — it defines the opposite of his purpose.

questions.question: Ferrante's final comment distinguishes his restaurant from a museum by suggesting that
options: A) food traditions can only survive if they are actively and regularly practised.
B) the restaurant charges lower prices than an educational institution would.
C) he plans to expand his work beyond the restaurant into schools and archives.
D) museums are not capable of preserving the cultural significance of food.
questions.explanation: Ferrante says 'to keep the thing alive, you have to keep cooking it', emphasising that active practice — not preservation in a static form — is what prevents traditions from dying.
